It is actually the plan for raised exceptions  to roll over and attend to, for example,  
- 107 = Transport endpoint is not connected

- 111 = Connection refused

- 113 = No route to host

These 3 cover essentially all errors for no gpsd,  wrong port,  no host.

Looking In the gpsd project, you'll see `fake.py` has a daemon check and a method to respawn an instance of gpsd, but with the introduction of systemd scripts I'm still trying to sort out a process viable in older POSIX systems and the new daemon handling systems.  I've not done that yet.

Error handling networked failures is another layer of complexity.

So, currently if it can't, it just tells you the problem rather than attempt 'fixing it'.

But, yeah, the client should roll with the punches,  rather than spit you out on the street.

> $PPWR,vv.vv,i,tt*hh<CR><LF>
> vv.vv is the modem supply voltage in volts.
> i is the ignition status: 0 = off; 1 = on.
> tt is the modem’s internal temperature in degrees Celsius.
> hh is the message checksum

> Example:
> $PPWR,14.12,1,43*37

@brettcannon pointed me here because we'd been chatting a bit about this on Twitter.

Yeah, hyper-h2 is exactly aiming at the place Brett speaks about when he asks "why the heck isn't there an HTTP parsing library?" (in fact, it's good to see people making this complaint, given that I'm speaking at PyCon SK 2016 and PyCon US 2016 about exactly this problem!).

Without going into too much detail and bogging it down, there *are* some HTTP parsers floating around Python-land (I published [pycohttpparser](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pycohttpparser) which is a wrapper around the C picohttpparser used in the H2O web server). However, none of these are as fully-featured as Hyper-h2.

I've considered writing something like Hyper-h2 for HTTP/1.1, and I may still do it sometime, but it's been a much lower priority for me than making Hyper-h2 as good as it can be. This is because, IMO, the ship has sailed with HTTP/1.1 for the foreseeable future. Even if I wrote a HTTP/1.1 parser/state machine, the reality is that it would be adopted by almost nobody. Requests/urllib3 would probably end up using it because we strongly dislike httplib/http.client (which we currently use, and aggressively work around), and if curio took off in a big way then maybe we'd land that too, but everywhere else the incumbent is already in place and doing just fine. For example, Tornado, Twisted, aiohttp, gevent, httplib, and any other HTTP/1.1 implementation in Python-land *already* went to the trouble to write themselves a HTTP/1.1 parser: why would they rip that out and replace it with a new thing?

With HTTP/2 I had the chance to get this right from the beginning because I was thinking about HTTP/2 two years before anyone else in the community started. This means the return on time invested is very high: why would anyone in the community write their own parser for such a complex protocol given that I've already spent thousands of man-hours on this one? For HTTP/1.1, the return on time invested is low, and may actually be smaller than 1: that is, I may spend more time writing and building the thing than anyone ever spends using it.

*With that said*, if someone decides this is the route they want to go *and* wants to aim for compatibility with Hyper-h2 (same event types etc.) then I am open to collaborating on the work and bringing it into the [Hyper organization](http://python-hyper.org/) as a companion to Hyper-h2.

Hi, 200 is only the default, and each method of the API that should return a different HTTP code should specify that. For example, when creating tickets `status=201` is passed in:

https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/blob/master/zdesk/zdesk_api.py#L1823

Zdesk not only looks for HTTP success codes, but uses the documentation to determine what code should be expected upon success. If it is anything other than the expected response, then we raise an error. This could either be an error in the documentation, or a corner case I'm unaware of.

You can get a complete list of all '_create' methods with something like this:

    awk '/def [a-z_]*_create/,/call/' zdesk_api.py

Then I can look at how many methods this is, and how many expect 201:

    $awk '/def [a-z_]*_create/,/call/' zdesk_api.py | grep def | wc -l
          61

    $awk '/def [a-z_]*_create/,/call/' zdesk_api.py | grep 201 | wc -l
          44

Looking at the remaining 17 '_create' methods, I see they are all getting the default of 200:

    $awk '/def [a-z_]*_create/,/call/' zdesk_api.py | grep call | grep -v 201 | less

    $awk '/def [a-z_]*_create/,/call/' zdesk_api.py | grep call | grep -v 201 | wc -l
          17

If you look at the documentation on, for example, `macro_create` at Zendesk, you can see that they do not have an "Example Response: Status: 201 Created" section, which is what my parser is looking for:

https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/core/macros#create-macro

Even though they have it in the "Using curl" section, I'm not parsing that block. So, the create methods missing the needed entry in the documentation are picking up the default of 200 and this needs to be addressed.

It's possible that every '_create' should expect a 201. If I can verify this with the 17 that aren't getting it, then that'd be the easiest fix. If not, I can either try to parse other parts of the docs, or make patches for the docs for these methods (ugh).

In the meantime, you can just go to the method and add `status=201` to the call for anything you find that is incorrect. For example, for `macro_create`:

https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/blob/master/zdesk/zdesk_api.py#L1284

    return self.call(api_path, method="POST", status=201, data=data, **kwargs)

And remember to include tests in pull requests. There's 2 kinds of tests here, one is Python unit test that calls the functions and compare the results. Another is to run pandoc directly and see if the output native AST is the same as a predefined one (usually generated automatically and just eyeballing to verify it's doing what it's supposed to do).

```diff
diff -ruN ldap3-0.9.8.2.bak/ldap3/core/server.py ldap3-0.9.8.2/ldap3/core/server.py

@@ -417,7 +417,7 @@
         candidates = []
         if addresses:
             if self.mode == IP_SYSTEM_DEFAULT:
-                candidates.append(addresses[0])
+                candidates = addresses
             elif self.mode == IP_V4_ONLY:
                 candidates = [address for address in addresses if address[0] == socket.AF_INET and (address[5] or address[5] is None)]
             elif self.mode == IP_V6_ONLY:
diff -ruN ldap3-0.9.8.2.bak/ldap3/strategy/base.py ldap3-0.9.8.2/ldap3/strategy/base.py

                         self.connection.server.current_address = candidate_address
                         self.connection.server.update_availability(candidate_address, True)
                         break
-                    except Exception:
+                    except LDAPSocketOpenError:
                         self.connection.server.update_availability(candidate_address, False)
                         exception_history.append((datetime.now(), exc_info()[0], exc_info()[1], candidate_address[4]))
 
@@ -159,7 +159,7 @@
         try:
             if self.connection.server.connect_timeout:
                 self.connection.socket.settimeout(self.connection.server.connect_timeout)
-            self.connection.socket.connect(self.connection.server.address_info[0][4])

             if self.connection.server.connect_timeout:
                 self.connection.socket.settimeout(None)  # disable socket timeout - socket is in blocking mode

A quick google search told me that you can use the tk `.see()` method to move to the end of a tkinter Text widget.

I knocked up a quick demo, each time text is added to the TextBox it called `TextBox.tk.see(END)`:

```python
from guizero import App, TextBox
from time import time
from tkinter import END

def add_text():
    tb.value = tb.value + str(time())    
    tb.tk.see(END)
    
a = App()
tb = TextBox(a, multiline=True, scrollbar=True, width=30, height=10)
tb.repeat(1000, add_text)
tb.line_no = 1
a.display()
```

Hey @Phantom-42, let's have a little discussion over here about the UX design of this PR. Currently you've implemented the following:

```
git gerrit request <project> create [--opts]
git gerrit request <project> fetch <ref>
```

usually, when adding a new service, what I start with are the basic operations:

* get a project (`clone`)
* create a new project (`create`),
* delete an existing project (`delete`),
* add service's remote to current project (`add`),
* fork the project (`fork`)

then I might play with some tougher APIs:

* list projects (`list`)
* handle snippets/gists (when supported)
* handle requests (`create`/`fetch`)

Now given the nature of gerrit, things might happen differently with it. But the questions are:

* how to match gerrit's workflow with the above toolbox, and 
* what would be missing to add in that toolbox?

I believe that feedback work (generally speaking, commenting/reading/acting on issues (#104) and PRs) is the next must-have feature of `git-repo`, but it's tough to actually design it right.

so as you're working on it, @Phantom-42, may I ask you for your opinion? (and @jayvdb as well, of course).

```
    def establish_connection(self, sleep_time=3, verbose=True, timeout=8,                           
                             use_keys=False, key_file=None):                                        
        """                                                                                         
        Establish SSH connection to the network device                                              
                                                                                                    
        Timeout will generate a NetMikoTimeoutException                                             
        Authentication failure will generate a NetMikoAuthenticationException                       
                                                                                                    
        use_keys is a boolean that allows ssh-keys to be used for authentication                    
        """                                                                                         
                                                                                                    
        # Convert Paramiko connection parameters to a dictionary                                    
        ssh_connect_params = self._connect_params_dict(use_keys=use_keys, key_file=key_file,        
                                                       timeout=timeout)                             
                                                                                                    
        # Check if using SSH 'config' file mainly for SSH proxy support (updates ssh_connect_params)
        if self.ssh_config_file:                                                                    
            self._use_ssh_config(ssh_connect_params)                                                
                                                                                                    
        # Create instance of SSHClient object                                                       
        self.remote_conn_pre = paramiko.SSHClient()                                                 
                                                                                                    
        # Load host_keys for better SSH security                                                    
        if self.system_host_keys:                                                                   
            self.remote_conn_pre.load_system_host_keys()                                            
        if self.alt_host_keys and path.isfile(self.alt_key_file):                                   
            self.remote_conn_pre.load_host_keys(self.alt_key_file)                                  
                                                                                                    
        # Default is to automatically add untrusted hosts (make sure appropriate for your env)      
        self.remote_conn_pre.set_missing_host_key_policy(self.key_policy)                           
                                                                                                    
        # initiate SSH connection                                                                   
        try:
            self.remote_conn_pre.connect(**ssh_connect_params)
        except socket.error:
            msg = "Connection to device timed-out: {device_type} {ip}:{port}".format(
                device_type=self.device_type, ip=self.host, port=self.port)
            raise NetMikoTimeoutException(msg)
        except paramiko.ssh_exception.AuthenticationException as auth_err:
            msg = "Authentication failure: unable to connect {device_type} {ip}:{port}".format(
                device_type=self.device_type, ip=self.host, port=self.port)
            msg += '\n' + str(auth_err)
            raise NetMikoAuthenticationException(msg)

        if verbose:
            print("SSH connection established to {0}:{1}".format(self.host, self.port))

        # Use invoke_shell to establish an 'interactive session'                                    
        self.remote_conn = self.remote_conn_pre.invoke_shell(term='vt100',width=110,height=1000)
        self.remote_conn.settimeout(timeout)
        self.special_login_handler()
        if verbose:
            print("Interactive SSH session established")

        time.sleep(.1)
        if self.wait_for_recv_ready_newline():
            return self.remote_conn.recv(MAX_BUFFER).decode('utf-8', 'ignore')
        return ""
```

Before telepot, I have not used any library's connection pooling facilities. But when I think about connection pooling, I envision these functionalities (and assume any libraries that support connection pooling to have them):

### It creates/re-uses/fixes connections as needed, transparently, within the connection limit. If a connection is idle for some (specified) time, it is closed. If a connection is broken, a new connection is tried.

Everything could be a refiner basically. What you need is the minimal information though, so you can "enrich" it from other sources.

You have to start with something. Given that videos are files, subliminal uses this fact to build an initial set of `Video` objects. Hence the `scan_video`, `scan_archive` and `scan_videos` functions.
One is free to build `Video` objects by other means and still can "enrich" them with refiners.

So basically if you want to start with something else than video files (not ideal IMO), you can't use the CLI and need to implement your own scanning mechanism based on whatever suits you. There may be a way to plug this with click in the current subliminal CLI but that seems like a lot of overhead.
The other solution is to let subliminal do the initial scanning and add your own refiner thanks to the `subliminal.refiners` entrypoint. Currently there is no way to specify refiners to use with the CLI but that's something I want to implement, the same way you can select your provider.

While a player's `name` (ie. Charles Johnson) and `gsis_name` (ie. C.Johnson) might not be unique, all players *do* have a unique id, `gsis_id`, assigned to them.

All player object have a `.playerid` that can be accessed, but `nflgame.find()` can assist in obtaining this `gsis_id` if you are searching for a specific name.  It can further be filtered by team for those cases where a non-unique name in the league exists.

```python
def find_playerid(name, team=None):
	if not nflgame.find(name, team):
		return 'No match'
	if len(nflgame.find(name, team)) > 1:
		print 'Could be either {}'.format(
			' or '.join([str(p) for p in nflgame.find(name, team)]))
		return [p.gsis_id for p in nflgame.find(name, team)]
	else:
		return nflgame.find(name, team)[0].gsis_id

def print_playerid(name, team=None):
	print 'Searching for "{}" (team = {})...'.format(name, team)
	return find_playerid(name, team)
```
```
Searching for "Calvin Johnson" (team = None)...
00-0025389
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Searching for "Charles Johnson" (team = None)...
Could be either Charles Johnson (DE, CAR) or Charles Johnson (WR, MIN)
[u'00-0025470', u'00-0030113']
Searching for "Charles Johnson" (team = MIN)...
00-0030113
Searching for "Charles Johnson" (team = CAR)...
00-0025470
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Searching for "Odell Beckham" (team = None)...
00-0031235
Searching for "Odell Beckham Jr" (team = None)...
No match
Searching for "Odell Beckham Jr." (team = None)...
No match
```
You'll notice that an exact match is required.  This is one area where `nfldb`'s fuzzy matching capabilities far exceed `nflgame`'s.

The unique id can (and probably should) be what you use to reference a player since even the player's `gsis_name` can change over time.  In fact, there are some players that the NFL fails to be consistent even from week to week.
```python
import nflgame
for week in range(7, 10):
	games = nflgame.games(2014, week, home='BAL', away='BAL')
	players = nflgame.combine_game_stats(games)
	for p in players:
		if 'S.Smith' in p.name:

I have also confirmed that what I thought was an unhandled exception in
pyodbc, was actually that I was too narrowly couching the except.  To
properly handle driver timeout exceptions simply change this:

import pyodbc
try:
        con1 =
pyodbc.connect('DRIVER={thedriver};SERVER='+(theserver)+';Port=theport;DATABASE='+thedatabase+';UID='+theuser+';PWD='+thepassword+'')
#...do other stuff
except pyodbc.DatabaseError: print("Could not connect to database.")

to this:

import pyodbc
try:
        con1 =
pyodbc.connect('DRIVER={thedriver};SERVER='+(theserver)+';Port=theport;DATABASE='+thedatabase+';UID='+theuser+';PWD='+thepassword+'')
#...do other stuff
except pyodbc.Error: print("Could not connect to database.")

Now all I need to do is figure out how to get other specific exceptions,
from failed cursor executions and the like, to print only their relevant
exception messages to stdout without the "Could not connect to database.

Thanks so much for all the help!

Hi @tentoseven - thank you for submitting your question.  Unfortunately, you're the first to be hit by a complexity of how the imports work vs. the contexts:

From the docs:

> import: import another test set file so you Don't Repeat Yourself

So, every import you run creates a new test set to be run (after the current one).  Each TestSet is intended to run as a separate test scenario, so it gets a fresh Context. Thus, while you can use imports to construct scenarios by writing to the database, the variables they write to the Context are lost after each run (as you've observed). 

I'm aware that this behavior limits the ability to use imports to create setup/teardown scenarios, and have plans on the future roadmap to fix this: https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/146  It may be reasonable as well to provide an override option for this behavior in the future as well (something like reuse_context_between_testsets?).  The latter is something that could be done easily enough in the next release. The former depends on architectural changes that I'm incorporating with the next 2 releases. 

For now, my recommendation is to do exactly as you are doing and include setup logic in each testset (even though this may cause some duplication). 

Does that answer your question and give some context?

Looking at issue #124 and poking at the code, this is my understanding of the problem:

1. Some arguments need escaping no matter their nesting level, e.g., directory names with spaces in them as @JoshRosen points out.  
2. For some arguments, we don't want escaping since we want the shell to expand them. E.g. '*.py'.
3. We don't want to shell-escape the same argument multiple times. Nesting three or more local commands is one way to trigger this. 
 
We need explicit control over argument quoting. One way would be to pass some arguments as a new type `RawString` or whatever rather than a native python string to tell plumbum we don't want any automatic escaping for that arg. That would remove a lot of the elegance of plumbum so that is probably not the way to go. Another option, which I'd personally vote for is to make the programmer responsible for automatically shell escaping strings. Existing packages such as [`pipes`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pipes.html) and [`shlex`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/shlex.html) (py3 only) provide a `quote` function fit for this purpose (at least for non-Windows platforms). 

Note, one can disable automatic shell-escaping for local commands _indirectly_ thru this ugly hack:

```python
import sys
from plumbum.machines import LocalCommand
LocalCommand.QUOTE_LEVEL = sys.maxint #any number >> 2 will do

make = local.get("make")
nice = local.get("nice")
print nice["-n", "19", make["VAR='A B C'", "test"]] 
# output: /usr/bin/nice -n 19 /usr/bin/make VAR='A B C' test
```

@rsennrich Thank you Rico for your clarification about those two approach. In my case the closest is BPE, but I'm not sure how to *pipe* it in the whole process, so the question.
I'm using multi-language models with Facebook FastText and other char-level CNN classifiers, FairSeq for NMT, and so the first problem was with the transliteration (in the unicode charset domain) of the source text (like Chinese, arabic, etc.). 

In this context, the `BPE` should be applied before the transliteration/uni-decode in order to work on the subword, and before any kind of normalization (newline, escaping, double quotes, quotes, etc.) so using this approach:

```
source text --- (BPE) ---> subwords ---> (UniDecode/Transliterate) ---> decoded text ---> (normalization) ---> output text
```

where the latter will populate the training set as well it will be used in the same process when doing inference.

Huh, I wonder what's going on with your network connection. Does magic-wormhole work for you from a different host (with a different network connection)? What does `pip list` in the virtualenv that you're using for magic-wormhole say?

Magic-wormhole is really only magical if you can use the singleton built-in server, otherwise you have to tell your correspondent about the server URL in addition to the wormhole code. That's why I haven't added anything like a config file to provides `--relay-url=` or `--transit-helper=`. Also, that localhost server is only going to be useful if both ends are running on your own computer (and running a server on your local LAN will only be useful if both ends are running in your own network). So I'm afraid that a local server isn't as useful as you'd like.

We've talked about having a way to detect other clients on a local LAN in the case that the standard global server isn't reachable (#48, #72), but we haven't implemented anything yet. The design issues are thorny.

This package can be extended in more general case.
In the function of summary, the inputs will be created automatically.
After the inputs are generated, the hook will record the information of each layer.
However, this design idea might limit the generalization.
Maybe ask the user to give the input is another approach.
Because the function just check the information of layer,
the correctness of tensor is not necessary. 
The user can just give the random value.
The following code shows my suggestion:
```python
from torch.autograd import Variable
from model import MyNetwork
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np
import torch

b, c, h, w = 32, 1, 28, 28

if __name__ == '__main__':
    input_1 = Variable(torch.from_numpy(np.random.random([b, c, h, w])).float())
    input_2 = Variable(torch.from_numpy(np.random.random([b, c, h, w])).long())
    model = MyNetwork()
    summary(model, input_list = [input_1, input_2])
```

To do this, you can just use the ```.fit()``` part of the ```KaplanMeierFitter``` then run the following

That will give me a better idea of whether a Weibull is okay.

After looking at your Kaplan Meier curve, I would like to again repeat my warning: I would be careful interpreting any 0% survival number you get out from any parametric model. Since your survival only goes to ~70%, we would do some serious extrapolation to get a survival time of zero.
Using this data as an example, 70% of the equipment failing by 1 year, then everything is censored at that point. All the equipment parts may fail in the next year after 3000 days. Alternatively all equipment may fail 100 years after 3000 days. Since you have no data for this time period, both are as equally likely to your data. A parametric distribution can overcome this, however it assumes the pattern in your data continues forward in time. It may not be reasonable to assume that

Having said all this, if you still want to extrapolate besides my warning and believe the Weibull distribution is okay, you can easily solve the following equation: ```S(t) = 0 = exp((lambda**rho) * (t**rho))``` for t. The command line prints the lambda and rho respectively for the Weibull. Since ```log(0)``` is undefined, we use a small value near 0 (I will use 0.001). Solving for t, we get ```t = (log(0.001) / -(lamba**rho))**(1/rho)```. Plugging in the numbers from your output, we get ~20385 days or ~56 years. Again, I would be suspicious of this estimate. Instead of all this math by hand, (I think) you can just run the following code
```python
from lifelines import WeibullFitter
wf = WeibullFitter()
wf.fit(T, E)
wf.survival_function_at_times(0.001)
```

We already handle a large number of errors and return meaningful error messages in the form of python Exceptions. For your app, you will need to use try/except to handle the error states that occur. Most of these errors will be `ValueError`s or `KeyError`s when specific data is not passed but is required, or the data passed does not match the required structure. These exceptions are raised before communication with the MailChimp API takes place to prevent errors from the output of that endpoint, though the errors will be sequentially triggered and fail on the first error. If anything fails at the MailChimp side after our error checking, we usually raise an HTTPError using the `raise_for_status` method to catch HTTP errors. 

Take a look at the code and method declarations for the API itself to see how different statuses are handled by the different operations [here](https://github.com/charlesthk/python-mailchimp/blob/master/mailchimp3/mailchimpclient.py).

I have a slightly different patch which I think works better? I couldn't get this to work with your patch for instance:

```bash
pip-custom-platform wheel --platform=macwat simplejson
pip-custom-platform install wheelhouse/*macwat.whl
```

(note that the way that `pymonkey` works (the monkeypatching utility library) is it's a *post* import hook.  It's too late to patch `pep425tags` because it has already initialized some module-scoped variables based on the real platform -- so I have to re-initialize them.  For pip10, this isn't necessary but that's not out / widely used yet).

Here's the patch:

```diff
diff --git a/pip_custom_platform/pymonkey.py b/pip_custom_platform/pymonkey.py
index 3d8e772..facf730 100644
--- a/pip_custom_platform/pymonkey.py
+++ b/pip_custom_platform/pymonkey.py
@@ -11,6 +11,11 @@ def pymonkey_patch(mod, args):
     if mod.__name__ == 'distutils.util':
         from pip_custom_platform.default_platform import get_platform_func
         mod.get_platform = get_platform_func(args, mod.get_platform)
+    elif mod.__name__ in ('pip.pep425tags', 'pip._internal.pep425tags'):
+        from pip_custom_platform.default_platform import get_platform_func
+        mod.get_platform = get_platform_func(args, mod.get_platform)
+        mod.supported_tags = mod.get_supported()
+        mod.supported_tags_noarch = mod.get_supported(noarch=True)
     elif mod.__name__ in ('pip', 'pip._internal') and hasattr(mod, 'main'):
         from pip_custom_platform._main import get_main
         mod.main = get_main(mod.main)
```

Ah, my thought is that you're running the environment without refining the action space (which is required as of 3.0.0 due to a new emulator in the back-end). Assuming you're looking for the standard action space _similar_ to what is in 2.3.1 (not exactly the same), you'd need to refine your template to include a `BinarySpaceToDiscreteSpaceEnv` wrapper from the `nes_py.wrappers` package and pass in the `SIMPLE_MOVEMENT` action list from `gym_super_mario_bros.actions`:

```python
from nes_py.wrappers import BinarySpaceToDiscreteSpaceEnv
import gym_super_mario_bros
from gym_super_mario_bros.actions import SIMPLE_MOVEMENT
env = gym_super_mario_bros.make('SuperMarioBros-v0')
env = BinarySpaceToDiscreteSpaceEnv(env, SIMPLE_MOVEMENT)

done = True
for step in range(5000):
    if done:
        state = env.reset()
    state, reward, done, info = env.step(env.action_space.sample())
    env.render()

env.close()
```

This is because the `nes_py` emulator allows the full range of 256 discrete actions on an NES joypad by default, and thus requires tuning to more realistic action spaces.

`gym_super_mario_bros.actions` defines three action lists (though you're welcome to create your own): 

| Name                                   | Movement      
|:-------------------------------------|:-----------------------------------------|
| `RIGHT_ONLY`                   | the agent can only run/walk/jump right
| `SIMPLE_MOVEMENT`      | the agent can do everything from: right only, walk left, and jump in place
| `COMPLEX_MOVEMENT`  | the agent can do everything from: simple movement, run/walk/jump left, and press down (enter pipes).

Hopefully this addresses your issue!

No, each time the client calls the `connect` function a new SID is generated.

> Is it possible to handle events from all namespaces in a handler for the default namespace?

There is no wildcard to handle all namespaces, but you can decorate a single handler function with multiple `@on()` decorators for multiple namespaces.

The second way, which I have made it work through the migration process, but haven't tested further was as follows:

class MeasurementField(FloatField):
    # ... some code skipped...

    def __init__(self, verbose_name=None, name=None, measurement=None,
                 measurement_class=None, unit_choices=None, *args, **kwargs):

        if not measurement and measurement_class:
            if type(measurement_class) != str:
                # Previous case - we probably should use the same logic that I used below
                # - i.e. module.measurement__class
                measurement = getattr(measures, force_text(measurement_class))
            else:
                # Some logic to recover the class with its full name with importlib (not ideal!)
                *module_name, cls = measurement_class.split('.')
                module_name = '.'.join(module_name)
                if module_name:
                    module = importlib.import_module(module_name)
                    measurement = getattr(module, cls, None)
                else:
                    # raise some error?
                    pass

        if not measurement:
            raise TypeError('MeasurementField() takes a measurement'
                            ' keyword argument. None given.')

        if not issubclass(measurement, self.MEASURE_BASES):
            raise TypeError(
                'MeasurementField() takes a measurement keyword argument.'
                ' It has to be a valid MeasureBase subclass.'
            )

        self.measurement = measurement
        # Storing the full name of the class, which is the only parameter that gets serialized
        self.measurement_class = force_text(measurement.__module__ + '.' + measurement.__name__)
        self.widget_args = {
            'measurement': measurement,
            'unit_choices': unit_choices,
        }

        super(MeasurementField, self).__init__(verbose_name, name,
                                               *args, **kwargs)

Thanks for the pull and getting ahead of the Marshmallow 3 stuff! (those dudes need to just release that already!)

So I have a couple concerns with the way this is implemented:

1. I think it makes sense to have the code "assume" the user is on marshmallow >= 3, but if they aren't, fall back to marshmallow2 compatible behavior. The current implementation is the opposite (assumes marshmallow 2, but falls back to marshmallow 3 / future behavior)

2. I don't think this is an actual problem in this particular scenario, but since one of the major motivations for refactoring efforts in marshmallow 3 were the result of greedily catching `AttributeError`.. so I'm a tad bit gun-shy of using `except AttributeError` when a marshmallow object is in question

I think there are a bunch of decent alternatives.. one way would be to inspect `marshmallow.__version__` - i.e. maybe `marshmallow.__version__.split('.', 1)[0] >= '3'` or something and change the behavior accordingly

Let me know if you think that makes sense!

The issue is that `to_csv` has changed signature between Pandas 0.13.1 and Pandas 0.16.2 (which is the current stable version)... In the version you are using, `to_csv` writes a file and requires additional arguments. In the latest version, `to_csv` returns a string when you call it without arguments.

See http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/0.13.1/generated/pandas.DataFrame.to_csv.html vs http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/0.16.2/generated/pandas.DataFrame.to_csv.html

@dogVgod Thanks for the logs.  From what I can tell, this is all tied to the fact that TensorFlow cannot write directly to HDFS (since setting `logdir=None` succeeds).  In my environment, this setting was critical for making this work:
```
--conf spark.executorEnv.LD_LIBRARY_PATH="/usr/local/cuda-7.5/lib64:$JAVA_HOME/jre/lib/amd64/server" \
```
And apologies, since it looks like the existing wiki instructions says to remove this line entirely for CPU mode.  Instead, you should try:
```
--conf spark.executorEnv.LD_LIBRARY_PATH="$JAVA_HOME/jre/lib/amd64/server" \
```

If that doesn't work, please try the following (since TF has some handling of `default`):
```
--model hdfs://default/user/${USER}/mnist_model
```

Meanwhile, I'll update the wiki...

Each example has it's own "scalability model", depending on how the code was originally written/structured.  Our goal with providing these examples was mostly to demonstrate the process of converting existing TF code to TFoS, and not to demonstrate re-writing the code to be more scalable.

So, the current examples' scalability models are:
- cifar10 - single-node, multi-gpu
- mnist - multi-node, single-gpu-per-node
- imagenet - multi-node, single-gpu-per-node
- slim - single-node, multi-gpu OR multi-node, single-gpu-per-node

Note that writing multi-GPU code takes a little bit of extra work (e.g. take a look at the cifar10 GPU "tower" architecture).  So, the simplest path is to go from single-node, single-gpu code to multi-node, single-gpu-per-node, since you're literally just running parallel instances of the same code in that case.

That all said, it's presumably possible to update the cifar10 example to be multi-node, multi-gpu-per-node (as you were originally attempting)... you'd just need to make some modifications to the code.

And, if you just want to try scaling out, I'd recommend using the imagenet example, since it's more compute intensive than mnist.  (This is what we measured on our original blog post).

There are several bugs in the code:

1. it doesn't consume basic.return's header and content frames; `BasicClass._recv_return()` is a NO-OP;  also, especially when in "publisher acknowledgements" mode, haigha needs to communicate to the user that a message was undeliverable, since basic.return(s) will, by design, precede the corresponding basic.ack.
2. Following the above problem, the rest of the frame processing logic gets confused. It expects the next frame to be method frame and tries to access its `method_id`, but it's actually basic.return's HeaderFrame. That triggers AttributeError
3. The above AttributeError causes `Channel.process_frames()` to call `Channel.close()`. At this point, the basic.return's ContentFrame hasn't been processed yet. Here, haigha violates AMQP stability rule for channel.close, and doesn't discard frames other than Close and Close-Ok, so it again trips up with AttributeError when it attempts to get `class_id` from the basic.returns's ContentFrame. The stability rule states:
  > After sending this method, any received methods except Close and Close-OK MUST be discarded. The response to receiving a Close after sending Close must be to send Close-Ok

4. The second AttributeError causes recursion into `Channel.close()`. At this point, there are no more channel frames coming from the broker, so `Connection.read_frames()` never returns. I don't know why Close-Ok is not arriving; in fact, I would have expected to basic.ack method frame following the basic.return sequence of frames, too.
5. Subsequently, when I hit Ctl-C, I would have expected the KeyboardInterrupt error (derived directly from BaseException) to propagate through all the layers and out of `read_frames`, but instead it ends up with `AttributeError: 'HeaderFrame' object has no attribute 'method_id'`. See the log traces below.

@apeforest I can’t share any code with you because I haven’t written it yet. However, I can explain the idea. One approach to reinforcement learning is to have a function that generates a probability distribution over all possible actions for each state, and then to take each action with the associated probability. If this probability distribution is generated by a softmax at the end of a deep learning model, we can optimize it by taking series of actions, measuring or estimating the rewards of each, and optimizing the distribution to maximize the expected reward. Temperature comes in because early in training, we have little information about the value of each action, so we want to intentionally bias the distribution to be more uniform, encouraging exploration. This can be achieved with a high temperature. As training continues, we can reduce the temperature, shifting probability mass to the most valuable actions, as we learn more about which actions are actually valuable. If there is a unique best action at each state, the zero-temperature limit will put all the probability mass on that best action, yielding an optimal trajectory.

There are other applications outside of RL as well; for example, temperature can be used in [model distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/1503.02531), i.e. training a simple model to mimic a complex one.

@safrooze  gave the solution in this [topic](https://discuss.mxnet.io/t/coordconv-layer/1394/4) in the discussion forum. The trick is to overwrite the forward function, and getting the layer shape in there. Example

```Python
from mxnet import gluon

class GetShape(gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self,nchannels=0, kernel_size=(3,3), **kwards):
        gluon.HybridBlock.__init__(self,**kwards)
        
        self.layer_shape = None
        
        with self.name_scope():
            self.conv = gluon.nn.Conv2D(nchannels,kernel_size=kernel_size)
            
            
            
    def forward(self,x):
        self.layer_shape = x.shape
        
        return gluon.HybridBlock.forward(self,x)
    
    def hybrid_forward(self,F,x):
        print (self.layer_shape)
        out = self.conv(x)
        return out

mynet = GetShape(nchannels=12)
mynet.hybridize()

mynet.initialize(mx.init.Xavier(),ctx=ctx)
xx = nd.random.uniform(shape=[32,8,128,128])
out = mynet(xx)
# prints (32, 8, 128, 128)
```

Hi @zakj. I was a bit mistaken in my description of the source of the duplication. You are totally right about the lock preventing two async flushes at the same time.

I actually encountered this this by making a *synchronous* flush (i.e. `consumer.flush(async=False)` using `AsyncBufferedConsumer`) while an asynchronous flushing thread was already in progress. Perhaps this is an issue more suited for the mixpanel-python-async project then.

Also as a side note, I just realized that my change would alter the behavior of the consumer if a MixpanelException is raised, by not preserving the buffer's contents in the event of the exception.

This is a great suggestion!

In general, we want to introduce as few hard dependencies as we can, and that includes the HTTP library. That said, we've been bitten by shortcomings of system HTTP libraries a *lot*, and would like to make sure that people have the option of using better tools where they're available and appropriate.

A workaround for this issue until it's fixed would be to write your own consumer that uses `requests` rather than `urllib2`. We would be happy to accept pull requests that make this easier to do (and I'm filing another issue for it now, #42)

Internally, we should consider attempting to import requests, and only using urllib2 if requests can't be found.

> Migrate is a tool to help the developer fix upgrade issues, but not all of them to restore the previous functionality

Where it doesn't generate false alarms and doesn't require crazy workarounds, we try to restore the old behavior in Migrate. That's usually easy with something like a removed API where we just put the removed code in Migrate.

It's not always possible to restore the old functionality because its behavior conflicts with the new functionality. (`jQuery.Deferred`s are an example.) If you have some jQuery 3.0 code that expects the new behavior, and some older plugin that was written for jQuery 2.0 that expects the old behavior, one of them is going to break. You might be able to "fix" it by including two versions of jQuery via `jQuery.noConflict()` but that's not a good solution. A compat flag in Migrate wouldn't solve it either and would just make things more confusing.

Thanks for looking at this. I guess I am trying to split things too much into small changes to them in. 

So here is the idea: `instance_init` is supposed to handle the *part of the initialization which may depend on the underlying HasTraits instance*. What `_setup_dynamic_initializer` does falls very much into that category. Hence, this should probably be called in `instance_init`.

One issue is that `instance_init` is also called by descriptors holding other descriptors like `List(Int())`, and we don't want these to be set up by dynamic initializers... Hence there should maybe be two levels for such initialization: one called at the top level only, i.e. in `__new__`, which does a bit more than the one also called for sub-traits.

```python
import requests

# First get zone_id and record_id using the official python api client.
# Then use Requests to delete the record.
headers = {
    'X-Auth-Email': CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL_ADDRESS,
    'X-Auth-Key': CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY,
    'Content-Type': 'application/json'
}
cloudinary_base_url = 'https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/'
url = "{0}/{1}/dns_records/{2}".format(cloudinary_base_url, zone_id, record_id)
response = requests.delete(url, headers=headers)

```

While this issue remains open, here is one way to distinguish the two requests by inspecting their headers:

1. The first request sets the request header to ```User-Agent = python/splinter```.  The code is [here](https://github.com/cobrateam/splinter/blob/c1c1cff513d60333e089b1a9a85d24c0099faf5a/splinter/request_handler/request_handler.py#L52).

2. The second request uses the browser's standard ```User-Agent``` (e.g. Mozilla, Chrome or whatever).

If you need to handle the first call separately server-side, you can simply inspect the request header to look for ```python/splinter``` and handle accordingly.  This isn't a solution to the issue, but may be a workaround until a solution is implemented.

The behavior of `resource_listdir()` in eggs is kind of surprising: It returns a list of filenames, and one of those filenames is the empty string.  From what I can tell, it's had this behavior since at least setuptools 5.0.

My change in #1232 doesn't change what `resource_listdir()` returns, but it does cause `resources_isdir('foo/')` to return True where previously it returned False for anything ending with a backslash.

The behavior of these functions is fairly underspecified, so I'm not sure what qualifies as feature or bug at this point.  The behavior of the egg and zip-based resources is supposed to match the behavior of filesystem based resources, and `os.path.isdir('foo/')` does return True.

If you want to revert my change #1232, that would be reasonable.  If you want me to change the behavior of `resource_listdir` to be a little less surprising, I could that instead, but there is undoubtedly tons of code that expects the old behavior.

>>> dateparser.parse('midnight last week')
datetime.datetime(2017, 8, 14, 0, 0)
>>> dateparser.parse('midnight last month')

Hi Stephen,

I really like this idea!

The bad news is that I don't know if we're going to be able to do it in the immediate future :/ The library is currently primarily targets Python 2, and we use "2to3" for compatibility across both major versions. I just ran some analytics against the Stripe platform, and requests from Python 2 outnumber requests from Python 3 by a full 10 to 1, so for the time being, this setup still makes sense for the project.

It might be possible to invert the primary major version by using something like "3to2" instead, but I suspect that it might not support really fancy Python 3+ constructs like async/await. Also, for now, [the asyncio docs seem to suggest avoiding the package if wide version support is necessary](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html):

> The async def type of coroutine was added in Python 3.5, and is recommended if there is no need to support older Python versions.

Given that Python's 2 to 3 chasm seems to be a problem that might never be solved, I suspect that a more plausible way to get this in would be start maintaining a separate Python 3 fork that can leverage the richer features of 3+. Unfortunately, I can't promise this anytime soon because we'd really need to ramp up out internal language teams to make it happen.

Even so, I'm going to leave this open and tag it as "future", and hopefully it'll become tractable given a little more time.

Thanks for the multimedia test examples.  One issue to ponder is how
to best deal with link attributes.

**(1)** The current test case describes a use case like the following:

```
"photos" [
    {
        "$ref": "cds://1800701.json"
    },
    {
        "$ref": "cds://1800701.json"
    }
]
```

where one would access referenced objects like this:

```
photos[0].title_statement_title
photos[1].title_statement_title

```

This works well for dereferencing "naked links".

**(2)** Now consider a situation where the link relation has parameters,
for example:

```
        +-------+                      +--------+
        | album |  <-------------->    | photo1 |
        +-------+   paramA, paramB     +--------+
          ref                           paramX, paramY

```

The parameters can be considered part of the linked object (`paramX`,
`paramY`) but also part of the relation itself (`paramA`, `paramB`).

How would one represent the relation with parameters?  Note that
[JSON Reference](http://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-pbryan-zyp-json-ref-00)
standard does not permit to have another field next to `$ref`, which
means one would have to introduce one more level:

```
"photos" [
    {
        'context': 'cover',
        '__link__': {
            "$ref": "cds://1800701.json"
        }
    },
    {
        "$ref": "cds://1800701.json"
    }
]
```

which not so consistent access technique:

```
photos[0].__link__.title_statement_title
photos[1].title_statement_title

```

**(3)** It may be better to use always-consistent linking and accessing
technique like:

```
"photos" [
    {
        'context': 'cover',
        '__link__': {
            "$ref": "cds://1800701.json"
        }
    },
    {
        '__link__': {
            "$ref": "cds://1800701.json"
        }
    }
]
```

and use the access methods explicitly:

```
photos[0].__link__.title_statement_title
photos[1].__link__.title_statement_title
```

even though this may appear a bit convoluted.  However, consistent
linking and accessing technique would win for me here.  (Principle of
least astonishment of sorts.)

**(4)** See also
[JSON Hyper-Schema](http://json-schema.org/latest/json-schema-hypermedia.html)
way of linking to other JSON records.

What did you thought of using in the multimedia album use case when
naked links aren't enough and one has to store some context attributes
of the relation?  Let's ponder pros/cons IRL?  CC @egabancho
@ludmilamarian

**P.S.** See similar musings for the authorities:

- https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-authorities/issues/1

We may want to profit from the same inter-linking technique for
linking records with parameters.

It sounds odd, but making small updates is currently a very difficult problem to solve.  This feature should absolutely exist, but we are not there yet. The Domo engine is designed to accept, standardize, and  _combine_ any amount of data from _hundreds_ of possible sources. We're always building it.

With that said, the API is just an extension of the product's current capabilities. Also, I understand the Execution suggestion - it would be nice to manage/update a large DataSet in slices. However, Executions are simply a record of an upload job, and don't bear any weight once they have been completed. 

In the future, I believe we will have a way of updating DataSets that would resemble a database UPDATE operation (specifying rows by ID or values). Don't quote me on that, but it makes perfect sense to have the ability to make small updates.

A bit more context about how I understand the situation thus far, because it may enable you to follow my train of thought so we can get to the bottom of what's going on here:

1. You created a symbolic link `/Users/reubano/bin/pip-accel3` which points to `/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/bin/pip-accel`.

2. When you run the `pip-accel3` command it will start a Python 3.5 interpreter to load the `pip_accel.*` modules which import the `pip.*` modules.

3. Because we started with a Python 3.5 interpreter the Python 2.7 module search path will be irrelevant, so `pip-accel` should definitely be importing the version of `pip` installed alongside `pip-accel` under `/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/site-packages`.

4. The version of `pip-accel` installed in the Python 3.5 environment should never even encounter the version of `pip` installed in the Python 2.7 environment because `pip-accel` never runs `pip` as a subprocess, it just imports the `pip.*` modules.

Just replying back. Let me know what you think:

>> What's the expected behavior if I try this on a resource that's not an @app.lambda_function()?

It would just accept the raw payload no matter what it is decorated with. So you would have to format the proper event payload if it is hooked up to an event source (i.e. s3 event, sns message, etc.). I think it would be interesting though if we can find a way to do preformatted payload as I mentioned in the initial overview. I'm just not sure how it would look, but I think no matter what it would be great to have the raw payload invoke.

>> Payload is required is it not?

Actually it is not from the SDK. Using the CLI on this ``app.py``:
```python
from chalice import Chalice

app = Chalice(app_name='hello-lambda')
app.debug = True

@app.lambda_function()
def index(event, context):
    print(event)
    return {'hello': 'world'}
```
I can run this command:
```
aws lambda invoke --function-name hello-lambda-dev-index saved-result
```
and it works fine without a payload. If omitted, an empty dictionary is provided.

>>  I think we can shorten it to just be a positional param. I would also expect a common use case to load the payload from a file. I'd almost think that's more common than specifying the JSON inline.

Yeah I would be fine with it being a positional argument as well. As to a file, I think that is nice especially if we are going to be allowing you to pass raw payloads for non-`app.lambda_function()` decorated functions. I would still like to have the option to pass it directly to the command line though for a couple of reasons:

1. It is a little annoying to save the JSON to a file first and then invoke the lambda function when the event payload is something small.

2. I do this in the tutorial I am building, but if I save a value to an environment/bash variable. Like I do [here](https://github.com/aws-samples/chalice-workshop/pull/14/files#diff-bcf8509328a78357c2a5dc3d30218972R253), I really cannot take advantage of the variable. I would have to echo it to a file and then use that file. That was just only my specific scenario, but I can imagine there are other examples where you want to use a functionality of the shell to construct the payload.

>> We should support -n/--name for the name arg, consistency with chalice logs -n

Makes sense to me.

>>  What about chalice local invoke?

I think that could make sense if we were just targeting it for local development. However, the primary way I am planning to use it is to invoke the remote lambda function. You can see an example in one of the PR's I was working on for the new chalice tutorial: https://github.com/aws-samples/chalice-workshop/pull/13. So my main concern is that it would be weird to have that under the `local` subcommand when this will be making a remote invocation.  Not sure if that changes your thoughts on that?

To 2. I understand that regulation may be outside of the scope of a metabolic model, however I think that all aspects of metabolism that can be represented should be represented explicitly (especially if it affects a model's results). I am yet to find a report for an organism without non-growth associated ATP costs, and it is fairly simple to determine experimentally. Thus, requiring it would certainly make sure that NGAM is considered by the user, so that in the next iteration the corresponding constraints will be applied.

To 3. If I understand the concept of NGAM correctly, it may depend both on the environmental and intracellular conditions. FWIW we use pants with multiple Django projects. They all live under `src/python`, so the source root gets autodetected correctly.  So, e.g., `src/python/mycompany/djangoproj1`, `src/python/mycompany/djangoproj2` and so on. 

So code in those projects imports like this:

```from mycompany.djangoproj1.models import *```

Instead of

```from djangoproj1.models import *```

So in that sense the projects "know" that they're under a shared top-level package.

Instead of cding into each dir, we do something like:

```./src/python/mycompany/djangoproj1/manage.py runserver```

from the root dir.   

Of course you have to have some way of ensuring all the deps are on your PYTHONPATH. We do this by creating a venv from our `3rdparty/python/requirements.txt` and creating a `./python` wrapper script that runs python in that venv (also adding `src/python` to the PYTHONPATH). 

So we can:

```./python src/python/mycompany/djangoproj1/manage.py runserver```

Or, since we modify manage.py's hashbang to `#! /usr/bin/env ./python`, we can just run

```./src/python/mycompany/djangoproj1/manage.py runserver```

In the future we intend to switch to

```./pants run src/python/mycompany/djangoproj1:manage -- runserver```

which is more idiomatic for pants. But we'd need pants startup time to be fast enough that this is equivalently zippy.

One way to fix this is eliminate a version and separate distribution of the binary altogether. Today we build the native engine in-line in the `./pants` script so it has a 1-1 mapping with current source code. If we switch to accessing the binary as a resource file instead of using BinaryUtil to fetch it, we could modify the current inline building `./pants` does to place the native engine binary file on the `PYTHONPATH` for access as a resource when developing pants. For releasing pants we'll need to embed the native binary in a platform-specific wheel, whether that be a wheel of `pantsbuild.pants` itself, or an auxillary resource-only wheel. Either way, the wheel will need to be prepared - if not deployed - by CI to ensure we uniformly get the desired OSX and linux variants.

Hi @wsokolow,

Be aware that we recently changed the default batch type to `word`; if you update sockeye to the latest version your default might change.

Yes, your comparison looks fine, but I wouldn't share your hypothesis that translation should be faster than training. In training, you use teacher forcing and thus can 'unroll' the computation over the entire target sequence. Especially for models like the Transformer this gives you heavily parallelized and fast computation, leading to high throughput.
In translation you have to do beam search and because of that additional logic, the computation is currently not pre-defined as in training. This is why we use the imperative API of MXNet there.
It is not surprising that translation is slower than training.

The argument you are referring to is just for evaluation of quality metrics (BLEU, Chrf). By default BLEU is a corpus-level metric, but you can use this flag to make `sockeye.evaluate` return sentence-level BLEU scores (smoothed).

Hi @DonaldTsang. Thanks for your suggestions!

Is there some particular aspect or feature of the IPFS WebUI that you think would be useful to communicate with from ipwb? 

While I think a browser add-on is currently out of the scope of our core efforts of ipwb, I think it might be useful as another means of communicating with the ipwb backend. We have considered integrating the preservation process and feel there are plenty of tools that already generate web archives. Integration with these tools to make the archiving-to-replay process more seamless has always been an ultimate goal. You may want to check out https://github.com/machawk1/warcreate for another tool that will allows preservation from the context of the browser (via a Chrome extension).

The concept of a native client is also interesting, as we want to remove technical barriers in potential users installing and using the tool. We originally did this with https://github.com/machawk1/wail as an example of generating a native binary from Python code, so are familiar with that process. However, because of issues of maintainability (among other reasons), @N0taN3rd rewrote the aforementioned app in Electron as https://github.com/n0tan3rd/wail.

No, the two are independent things. We can continue implementing support for `Prefer` header for raw memento. However, with client-side IPFS fetch, we will not need either of the raw or rewritten mementos as we will be performing the composition on the client side directly. Those accessing the server without the SW in place would need to talk to the regular memento endpoint.
That is not called TimeMap and there is no name for it as such.

Clearly, if you're going to run with errorabend, it will be completly expected that the sub-process terminated, and I'm still inclined to keep the existing message in the LOG value of the returned dictionary.

I was going to suggest that you use the saslog() method (off the SASsession object) as that will have as much log that could be gotten. But, I see that when I catch the sub-process is gone, I'm not trying to pull anything left off the LOG's pipe. I just accounted for that in my code and I was able to retrieve the last of the log, and append it to the full log you get from sas.saslog(). I can't say that will will always work; could be some timing constraints as the pipes are closed when the subprocess terminates, so it might not work 100%. I've set up a repo where I put all my Windows-compatible exempi directory : https://github.com/MaxyMoos/exempi-Win7-VS2013/

Here are the steps that lead to a successful compilation on my machine:
Everything was built with Visual Studio Express 2013 on Windows 7 (64-bit), so there might be some instructions that apply only to this specific version:

**1-** In _/build/shared/CMakeUtils.bat_, change

`set GeneratorVersion = Visual Studio 11` **to** `set GeneratorVersion = Visual Studio 12` (line 25)

and (lines 62 to 64):
```
if /I "%1"=="2011" (
echo "Generator VS 2011 specified"
set GeneratorVersion=Visual Studio 11
```
to:
```
if /I "%1"=="2013" (
echo "Generator VS 2013 specified"
set GeneratorVersion=Visual Studio 13
```

**2-** Add the zlib (1.2.8) headers and expat (2.1.1) lib folder in _/third-party/zlib/_ and _/third-party/expat/_

**3-** In /build, execute the GenerateXMPToolkitSDK_win.bat tool and use option 2 (_Generate XMPSDKToolkit Dynamic Win32_). The cmake build should now work and Visual Studio solutions should have been generated for XMPCore & XMPFiles.

**4-** Beginning the fun part: there are some modifications to be done in order to compile the XMPFiles solution:

In _XMPFiles/source/FileHandlers/JPEG_Handler.cpp_ :
Add `#include <algorithm>` after all the other include instructions (else there's a conflict for the definitions of std::min and std::max)

And we need to add a few files that are missing from the project...
In the XMPFiles project, open the Solution Explorer and under XMPFiles / Header Files / Internal Headers:
* Add under File Handlers: GIF_Handler.hpp
* Add under Format Support: GIF_Support.hpp

And under XMPFiles / Source Files :
* Add under File Handlers: GIF_Handler.cpp
* Add under Format Support: GIF_Support.cpp

**5-** Compile both XMPFiles and XMPCore solutions

**6-** Create a new project _libexempi_ in VS and add the following files from the _/exempi_ folder to it:
- exempi.cpp
- xmp.h
- xmp++.hpp
- xmpconsts.h
- xmperrors.h

In the project properties, add to the Include folders:
* The pthread headers directory
* The XMPCore and XMPFiles source directories
* The exempi root directory
* The /public folder of the exempi root dir

Add to the linker libraries folders:
* The pthread lib directory
* The /public/libraries/windows/Release directory, where your XMPCore and XMPFiles DLLs and .lib must have been generated

Add to the linker's _Additional dependencies_ :
* XMPFiles.lib
* XMPCore.lib
* pthreadVSE2.lib

Finally, in /public/XMP_Environment.h, add the following at the top of the file:
`#define WIN_ENV 1`

And last but not least: add `DllExport` around all functions in xmp.h and exempi.cpp.

2. I encountered a problem with the CompareDateTime function call (linker issue that I didn't manage to solve), so to work around it, I duplicated all the necessary code in exempi.cpp

Don't forget to set the project configuration to generate a DLL and not a .exe or static lib and normally, you're good to go.

[conda_install_issue.txt](https://github.com/conda/conda/files/320801/conda_install_issue.txt)

`install.packages()` is tricky. A large part of the point of `Anaconda Distribution` is to build binary packages for you that are well tested and known to work (as well as being secure and of high performance).

Using `install.packages()` cannot guarantee any of that; rather, on Linux, it puts you at the mercy of your system package manager and the compilers you have on your `PATH` and the compatibility of those with software compiled with the `Anaconda Distribution` compilers. On OSes other than Linux, replace "system package manager and the compilers you have on your `PATH`" with "binaries available on CRAN".

I've been tempted to re-route `install.packages()` through `conda`, perhaps for now hidden behind an env. var that'd be disabled by default but I'm not sure. One thing I have done is to make R 3.4.3 depend upon our compilers so that `install.packages()` has a better chance of success.

In general, regarding user package dirs for R and Python, maybe we should consider and env. var that controls whether this is used or not? Not sure whether we'd used the same one for both ecosystems or not?

> On the other hand, Kale, @mbargull, and I have been discussing the notion of "strict" channel priority. In this mode, conda would not even consider packages in a lower-priority channel if they exist in the higher-priority one. So for instance, for users that put conda-forge over defaults, conda would never reach into defaults for packages that live in both.

I believe @ocefpaf has asked about something along these lines in the past.  I can imaging a few cases where this would break but I suspect that such breakage might actually be desired.  

For example, if the highest priority channel had a package `spam` which required `eggs` version 2.0 but only provided `eggs` versions 1.0, "strict" channel priority would result in an unsatisfiable error.  This could be see as a good behavior as it alerts the channel maintainers about the lack of a `eggs` version 2.0 package.  On the other hand if a lower priority channel had `eggs` 2.0 this "strict" channel priority is preventing this channel to fulfill the requirement.  

Could the "strict" channel priority be expanded to exclude only packages with the same name AND versions in lower priority channels?

The issue here can be reproduced in a much simpler context (code in Python 3).

    >>> b"sdkfasdkfjasld\nasdsadfasdf          ".strip().split('\n')
    Traceback (most recent call last):

Hi @bioinformed and @kyleabeauchamp. The issue was that the travis script ran:

```
python setup.py sdist
...
pip install pysam-xxx.tar.gz
```

However, the dist command does not cythonize .pyx files into .c. The fix was to run:

```
python setup.py build sdist
...
pip install pysam-xxx.tar.gz
```

I am not sure why it used to work. Could be that .c files were accidentally part of the repos or this might be change with the cython 0.25 builder?

Hi @msalvadores, unfortunately I do not have a great solution for this issue yet.

Changing the logging level for all 404s is not ideal, because we have plenty of scenarios where this error is valid and should be logged as an error. I agree that in the case of the `exists` call, it is not really an error since it was expected. I'll look into ways to handle this, but right now I do not see any simple elegant solution that does not require some re-architecture of the executor.

In the meanwhile, if you really do not want 404s to be logged as errors, I would suggest to write your own [filter](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#filter) to throw them out, or convert their level to info. 

I'll keep this PR open to track the issue, thank you so much for bringing it to our attention!

ok, here's a few ways you _could_ do this...

Note again that client side hooks are intended as a developer tool and not as a stopgap, you really need some other tool to actually _enforce_ your checks (whether that's CI, a server-side `pre-receive`, or however else you actually want to do it).

But first, a comment about micro vs. macro repositories.  If you find yourself with hundreds of repositories (in theory to avoid a monorepo / monolith / etc.) you're really doing yourself a disservice if you don't have a way of applying a mass change in an automated way.  This was incredibly frustrating for myself when I as a young padawan worked at a company that switched from a single monolith repository to a heavily service oriented pattern.  My thought process went something like: "yay! freedom! agility!.... wait, everything is bitrot and version skew now".  Fortunately, there's usually good solutions to these types of things -- in my case I decided to build a tool which acted as a distributed automated changeset applier which (again) made sweeping changes painless.  (_an aside_: personally I'm _pro_ micro-repos and _anti_ monorepos, but your mileage may vary).  This tool for the longest time lived entirely in closed source and was site specific -- but fear not, I've rewritten it from scratch and it works great for my use-cases: [asottile/all-repos](https://github.com/asottile/all-repos).  all-repos actually comes out-of-the-box with tools to [help manage `pre-commit`](https://github.com/asottile/all-repos#example-autofixer) as samples for autofixers.  I also have a running curation of rewrites I've done on my public repositories here: [asottile/all-repos-incantations](https://github.com/asottile/all-repos-incantations).

ok, advertising over -- let's get down to business :)

A few things to get out of the way first though, these are mostly design decisions taken by pre-commit that I'm not willing to change (you can find similar texts in the rest of the issue tracker but I'll rewrite them here).
- `pre-commit` _must_ have a configuration to run.  pre-commit itself is a framework and will not grow opinions about what hooks you should use or how you should use them.  If you want to run it, you need to have some sort of configuration :)
- `pre-commit` will not directly support mutability in configuration (this includes referencing a centralized repository).  This causes lots of issues with repeatability and maintenance in general.  This was an early design decision after seeing the pain caused at scale when a linter changes behaviour and suddenly every repository's _master_ branch is broken.
- One of `pre-commit`'s goals is to make local development _better_ by providing feedback _as early as possible_.  If you're able to run a tool locally before pushing it off to CI or a `pre-receive` hook and easily verify / validate the changes (even automatically!) you're going to be much happier.  Again, this was an early design decision around the frustrations of server-side hooks that were confusing, slow, and sometimes difficult to reproduce locally.  This is why pre-commit (and the current `git` hooks it targets) are aimed at _client_ hooks.

And now let's get on to the ways you can hack this behaviour on (disclaimer: while I may disagree with some of these ideas, they do currently work) :)

- [nested pre-commit configurations](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/731#issuecomment-376945745)
- nothing is stopping `.pre-commit-config.yaml` from being a symlink! (if you control all the host machines you could presumably "puppet out" a configuration to a well known location and check in a symlink to it
- on the topic of symlinks,  I've seen some repositories maintain their shared configuration in a centralized repository added as a submodule and then symlinks into that (you'd still have the problem of _updating_ that submodule, but that's easy to automate).
- specifically for _user_ specific hooks, you could take advantage of the ["migration mode"](https://pre-commit.com/#running-in-migration-mode) for pre-commit (the docs don't discuss the implementation details but they're not changing any time soon: `pre-commit` will [invoke a hook by the name of `$HOOK_TYPE.legacy`](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/259ef9e53041b43e746da5b2b1fe5ca9020d70fe/pre_commit/resources/hook-tmpl#L54-L59) if it exists.  With this you could set up a shell script that invokes `pre-commit run --config ~/my-config.yaml` or some sort.
- You could install your own shell script period and not use `pre-commit install` and use the same idea.
- If you control the server and can install server-side hooks, pre-commit supports [`--origin` and `--source`](https://pre-commit.com/#pre-commit-run) for running against commit ranges

These are just a few ideas, sorry if this is rambly and incomplete (have to run now!)

@quasiben There are a couple of things going on here.

I'll start by mentioning that operating on standalone fields (that are not part of any model) is not really well-established usage. Up until now, standalone fields have been used in test cases but little more.

Recently, however, I've been adding bits and pieces to make it possible to use a standalone type as the root element. I can definitely see value in that usage. Sometimes you just need to coerce or validate a list of items, for instance. The items themselves may be complex, but there just isn't a model-like structure at the root.

For the moment, this only has experimental support, since there are no comprehensive tests yet.

Anyway, congrats — you found 2 bugs. One had to do with what I explained above; a standalone `ListType` would not import a nested `ModelType`. The other was a bona fide regression in `MixedType`.

Both have now been fixed in the `mixed-type` branch, and your sample code should work. But note that `MixedType` has now been renamed `UnionType`.

Wow, we have a lot of options.

There's several categories of options here.

1. Options which are their own subcommands, such as  --print-pre, --print-rdf, --validate
2. Options which affect what documents can be loaded, such as --non-strict, --enable-dev
3. Options which affect management of the workflow, such as --cachedir, --force-docker-pull
4. Options that affect the actual runtime environment of workflow tools, such as --enable-net/--disable-net, --preserve-environment, --eval-timeout

It doesn't really make sense for the first two categories of options to be workflow requirements, because they affect the handling of the workflow document itself.

The third category of options could become hints.  However, because they tend to be site specific, they should be provided via an "override" separately from the workflow document.

The fourth category of options are things that were added as compromises (CWL doesn't specify X, user needs X, so make it available as a non-standand cwltool behavior), these definitely need to be removed from the command line and specified as hints/requirements.

Yes, your understanding is correct.

As far as python-dev is concerned, it's legit to install both versions. The difference is very simple - the x86_64 architecture lets you address more memory (so your programs can have a heap bigger than 4G) at a cost of larger pointers (which means your memory usage grows by a factor of up to 2, depending on your use case).

Generally speaking, x86_64 *should* be faster with new registers and whatnot but in practice it never is because of the cache impact of larger pointers and clever tricks that let the processor optimize 32bit programs.

Hi Shimin,

Are you talking about the % of Reads Identified on the SAV Indexing Tab? It sounds like you are, but I just wanted to make sure. 

We don't average it for the run because some of the flowcells for certain sequencers have individually addressable lanes, and we don't have a fool proof way to determine whether the flowcell has the same set of samples in each lane.

For your application if you know this is not the case, then you can just average the numbers over all lanes.

@sidps I'm not familiar with log-softmax, is that different from softmax w/ cross entropy loss? Or do you mean a layer that does log(softmax(x))? If it's the latter, what loss function do you pair that layer with?

> although I suppose it has other use cases besides autoencoders

Hmm, but note that this layer can only be used to unpool / deconvolve if you've also got a layer that pools / convolves something of the shape you want to output. So it's a bit trickier to use outside of a encoder/decoder architecture, although it should be possible in general. (It will get horribly inefficient if the layers you want to invert are neither part of the forward pass nor their underlying Theano ops all support shape inference, but it would still work.)

I mainly like `autoenc.py` because, off the top of my head, that's the only category that we could put it into that might attract additional layers and macro functions. We could also put it in `special.py` and move it somewhere else as soon as it makes sense. `special.py` could be a container for all layers that would stay alone in any more specific category for the foreseeable future.

The functionality is definitely all already there, yeah. The idea of this branch is to give a very easy-to-use API for new users of angr. As the Manticore README wisely points out, while angr is very powerful, we could do better in being approachable. With the hooking decorator and the `project.execute` functionality that's on this branch, we can now do:

```
import angr

project = angr.Project("angr-doc/examples/defcamp_r100/r100", auto_load_libs=False)

@project.hook(0x400844)
def print_flag(state):
    print "FLAG SHOULD BE:", state.posix.dump_fd(0)
    project.terminate_execution()

The idea is probably to have similar to our stub.py:  since it would be trickier to overload name-spaces, I would say that "stock" duecredit matlab/octave module would provide filenames with `_` in them, e.g. `duecredit_cite_.m` and then we will provide the ultimate "stub" module `duecredit_cite.m` which people would copy to their code-base to carry around and which will have the
```
try
    duecredit_cite_('GNU Octave')
catch
    % do nothing
end
```
not sure exactly what to do with different types of citation (safetly catch all for which we provide within the same stub.py - BibTeX, Doi, etc) but I guess we could easily just make duecredit_cite as first argument accept a string which would state what kind of reference next argument is (bibtex, doi, url, etc). So we end up with only 1 file

2. "Does that mean that duecredit for Matlab/ Octave would always require that Python"  At the beginning -- I think so.  But we will make duecredit available from everywhere possible (we have it on pypi already) -- standalone bundle, conda, etc, may be even we could provide some ugly `duecredit_install.m` to be shipped along to install a standalone bundle on a given system.  Later we might cook up a duecredit.org website, to which folks could upload their citations manually or may be even that `datalad_cite.m` could get a basic implementation to upload those collected citations.  I don't think it is worth reimplementing everything in matlab/octave

3. Injectors -- primary motivation for them is to demonstrate benefit of duecredit this early in its life-time.  I hope that eventually projects just adopt duecredit stub/citations within their code base so no injections would be necessary.  Indeed messing up with path in matlab would be a cruelty better to avoid.  And in Matlab land if e.g. cosmomvpa, eeglab, and few others adopt it -- that hopefully would provide sufficient demo/motivation for others.
